title: SEGMENT 15 — PacBio Iso-Seq: full-length transcript sequencing, isoform discovery

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — PacBio Iso-Seq: full-length transcript sequencing, isoform discovery

Let's spend a segment on a PacBio application that doesn't get as much press as genome assembly, but which is uniquely powerful and uniquely PacBio: Iso-Seq, the platform's full-length transcript sequencing workflow.
Here's the motivation.
RNA, in eukaryotic cells, is processed from primary transcripts into mature mRNAs through splicing — the cutting out of introns and the joining of exons.

Most genes produce multiple distinct mRNAs through alternative splicing — different combinations of exons joined together in different ways, producing protein isoforms with subtly different functions.
A typical human gene has, on average, four to ten major isoforms, and many genes have dozens.
Knowing which isoforms a gene produces, in which tissues, at which developmental stages, is fundamental for understanding what the gene actually does.
Short-read RNA-seq, the standard tool, doesn't see isoforms directly.
It sees short fragments of transcripts — 150-base reads from random positions along the mRNA — and the isoform structure has to be inferred from which exon junctions are observed in the reads.
This inference works for simple cases but fails for genes with many isoforms, especially when isoforms differ only at distant points along the transcript.
You see lots of evidence for various splice junctions, but you can't tell which combinations of junctions actually co-occur on the same molecule.
The full isoform structure is a guess.
Iso-Seq solves this directly.
A single HiFi read can span a complete transcript from 5' cap to polyA tail.
You see exactly which exons are joined together on that molecule.
No inference.
The isoform is observed as a single piece of data.

The Iso-Seq workflow takes total RNA, converts it to full-length cDNA using a clever oligo-dT-and-template-switching method that captures complete transcripts, builds these into SMRTbells, sequences with HiFi chemistry, and outputs full-length transcript reads.
Each read is a single mature mRNA, in full, from one end to the other.
Tens of thousands of distinct transcripts can be characterized in a single run.
The applications are powerful.
Isoform discovery in new species — for any organism whose transcriptome hasn't been thoroughly characterized, Iso-Seq is the tool of choice.
Cancer-specific isoforms — tumors often produce aberrant transcripts (gene fusions, exon skipping events) that Iso-Seq can directly characterize.
Tissue-specific isoforms — Iso-Seq across tissue types reveals which isoforms are expressed where.
Developmental dynamics — Iso-Seq across developmental stages reveals isoform switching that drives differentiation.
A specific contrast with short-read RNA-seq.
Suppose a gene has three isoforms: ABCD, ABD, and BCD (where each letter represents an exon).
Short reads can see junctions A-B, B-C, C-D, B-D, A-C — but it cannot tell whether a read crossing A-B and a read crossing C-D came from the same molecule.
The assignment of junctions to isoforms is statistical, and for genes with many junctions, the answer can be ambiguous.
Iso-Seq reads, by contrast, directly observe each molecule from A through D, and the isoform identity is read off in one piece.
ABCD is ABCD; you don't have to infer it.
Iso-Seq is therefore the gold standard for any work that requires accurate isoform-level resolution.

It's been used to build comprehensive isoform catalogs for human tissues, to discover novel isoforms in disease contexts, to characterize complex transcriptomes in non-model organisms, and to validate isoform predictions from short-read pipelines.
Nanopore offers a similar capability, sometimes called direct RNA sequencing in their version, which has its own appeal (no cDNA conversion).
But for accurate, full-length, quantitative isoform sequencing in the standard mode, PacBio Iso-Seq is the dominant tool today.
